topic: Arizona Governor Wins Primary Setting Race

left.title: Trump ally wins Republican primary for Arizona governor, setting up race against Katie Hobbs

left-center.title: Rep. Andy Biggs wins GOP primary for Arizona governor

center.title: GOP's Andy Biggs' Chances of Defeating Katie Hobbs in Arizona Governor Race

right-center.title: Trump-backed firebrand wins Arizona GOP governor primary, setting up high-stakes showdown

farLeftText:
Arizona voters on Tuesday chose their nominees for governor, Congress and other top offices, setting up a fall campaign that will test whether Republicans can reverse some of the Trump-era losses they suffered in a premier battleground state.

Andy Biggs, the hard-right congressman endorsed by Donald Trump, easily won the Republican nomination to challenge the incumbent Democrat, governor Katie Hobbs.

The race is expected to be among the most fiercely contested in the country, with control of the governor’s office carrying outsized national significance in a state that became “ground zero” for election denialism after Trump’s narrow loss there in 2020.



Biggs, a former chair of the House freedom caucus who was a central figure in the effort to overturn Trump’s election defeat, trounced fellow congressman David Schweikert, by appealing to the party’s deeply conservative base.

Throughout the campaign, Biggs and Schweikert offered constrasting visions for reclaiming the governorship. Schweikert, who gave up his toss-up congressional seat to run for governor after eight terms, argued that Biggs’ hard-right politics and unswerving embrace of the president had allowed Democrats to make gains in once-ruby-red Arizona.

View image in fullscreen Andy Biggs, who is running in the Arizona governor primary, speaks at a campaign event on 17 July. Photograph: Ross D Franklin/AP

But Republican voters in Arizona, as they have across the country, followed the president’s lead, even though Trump’s candidates have repeatedly fallen short in the state’s most competitive general election contests.

Businessmen Ken Miceli and Scott Neely were also vying for the nomination. Hobbs, who flipped the seat from red to blue in 2022, ran unopposed.

Arizona has transformed in recent years from a Republican stronghold into a competitive swing state, fueled by rapid population growth, shifting suburban voting patterns around Phoenix and an influx of college-educated voters.

In the decade since Trump’s election in 2016, Democrats have gained control of the top three statewide offices – governor, attorney general and secretary of state – as well as both US Senate seats. Trump narrowly carried Arizona in 2024 after losing the state four years earlier – a defeat he unsuccessfully sought to overturn.

This year, Republicans are contending with unfavorable national political conditions. Historically, the president’s party loses ground in the midterm elections, and Democrats are hopeful they can seize control of the House – and possibly the Senate, riding a wave of discontent over high gas prices and an unpopular Iran war that have dragged down Trump’s approval ratings. Two toss-up House districts in Arizona are key to that effort.

View image in fullscreen Arizona governor Katie Hobbs looks on as she speaks to press during the opening of Copper State Victory’s first coordinated Democratic campaign office in Tucson, Arizona on 12 July. Photograph: Rebecca Noble/Reuters

Arizona’s first congressional district, anchored in affluent Scottsdale, has long been a Democratic target as the suburbs shifted away from the Republican party over the past decade.

Two years ago, Schweikert won re-election with just under 52% of the vote, narrowly defeating Amish Shah, a former state lawmaker who Democrats appeared poised to re-nominate on Tuesday.

His challenger, the former local news anchor Marlene Galán-Woods, also sought the nomination two years ago. This year, Galán-Woods, the widow of former Republican attorney general Grant Woods, who publicly broke with his party over Trump, earned the controversial backing of the Democratic congressional campaign committee.

Shah will face Republican Jay Feely, a former Arizona Cardinals kicker, in November. Feely faced Joseph Chaplik, a former state lawmaker, and John Trobough, a technology and telecommunications executive, in his party’s primary.

In the sixth congressional district, which extends from the suburbs of Tucson to the US-Mexico border, encompassing much of south-eastern Arizona, Democrats hope national headwinds facing the president’s party will dislodge Republican congressman Juan Ciscomani, who is seeking a third term. Democrat JoAnna Mendoza, a Marine Corps veteran, was running unopposed.

Meanwhile, the contest to succeed Biggs in the safely Republican fifth congressional district has drawn unusual attention. The Trump-backed Republican nominee, former Pinal County Sheriff Mark Lamb, spent the closing weeks of the primary campaign confronting allegations of sexual misconduct and abuse of power reported in a multi-part investigation by the Arizona Republic. Prosecutors found no basis for criminal charges, and Trump has re-affirmed his support for Lamb, who has denied wrongdoing.

He will face Democrat Elizabeth Lee, a nurse, in the general election.

View image in fullscreen Republican candidate for Arizona attorney general Warren Petersen Photograph: Rebecca Noble/Reuters

Elsewhere, Republicans chose their nominees for two top state positions that Democrats see as critical to pushing back against the Trump administration.

Warren Petersen, president of the Arizona senate, was leading the Republican primary race to take on Democrat Kris Mayes for attorney general. Mayes, an outspoken critic of Trump, won the office in 2022 by just 280 votes after an automatic recount, making it one of the closest statewide races in Arizona history. He was competing for the nomination against Rodney Glassman, a former Democrat who was the party’s Senate nominee against John McCain in 2010 before joining the Republican party.

View image in fullscreen Alex Kolodin won the Republican nomination for Arizona secretary of state. Photograph: Rebecca Noble/Getty Images

Alex Kolodin, a Republican state representative and attorney who helped lead failed legal efforts to overturn Arizona’s 2020 and 2022 election results, will face Democratic secretary of state Adrian Fontes, in November for the office that will oversee elections in a swing state during the 2028 presidential race. Fontes has cast himself as a bulwark against Trump’s election denialism while overseeing elections in one of the nation’s most closely watched battleground states.

Kolodin defeated Gina Swoboda, a former state party chair who abandoned an earlier bid for Schweikert’s House seat to instead run for secretary of state.

Tuesday’s primary also settled tight contests in legislative races across Arizona, where Republicans hold slim majorities in both chambers of the state capitol.

centerText: Governor Katie Hobbs holds a polling lead in the Arizona gubernatorial race over Representative Andy Biggs, who won the Republican nomination to challenge the Democratic incumbent in the key swing state. Biggs won the Republican primary with roughly 70 percent of the vote Tuesday night, setting him up for the November election. Hobbs was first elected in 2022 and has built an early polling lead, despite President Donald Trump carrying Arizona by more than 5 points in the 2024 election. The Arizona governor race is expected to be among the most competitive of the 2026 midterm elections. Democrats are optimistic about Hobbs’ polling lead and believe Trump’s declining approval rating could drive a 2018-style blue wave that boosts Democratic candidates across the country. Read More on News But Republicans note that Arizona was Trump’s strongest swing state in 2024, marking a rightward shift in a state that had become more competitive across prior election cycles. Katie Hobbs vs. Andy Biggs: What Polls Show Hobbs held a lead over Biggs in the latest polls of the governor race, though the latest data suggests the race will still be close. A Noble Predictive Insights poll showed her leading Biggs 41 percent to 37 percent. Hobbs’ net favorability stood at +9 points compared to Biggs’ +5 points. But more Arizonans were unsure of their feelings toward Biggs—14 percent said they had no opinion or never heard of Hobbs, compared to 37 percent for Biggs. ... Hobbs’ lead was bolstered by a strong performance among self-described moderates, 48 percent of whom said they would vote for her compared to 20 percent for Biggs. The poll surveyed 996 registered voters from May 5 to May 7 and had a margin of error of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points. A TIPP Insights poll, which was conducted from April 20 to April 24 among 1,159 likely voters, showed Hobbs up by double digits. She received 48 percent support to Biggs’ 38 percent in that poll. An earlier Emerson College poll pointed to a much closer race—Hobbs’ support stood at 43 percent, compared to Biggs’ 43 percent. That survey was conducted among 850 registered voters from November 8 to November 10. It had a margin of error of plus or minus 3.3 percentage points. Hobbs’ advantage stems from the fact that she is an incumbent governor who has had to work with a conservative state legislature, Thom Reilly, professor of public affairs and co-director of the Center for an Independent and Sustainable Democracy at Arizona State University, told Newsweek. “This has moderated her, in the sense that she has had to work with the legislature in order to balance the budget and in order to get some key legislation,” he said. He pointed to independents as a key voting bloc that will shape the outcome of the race, noting that neither candidate can win without them. It can be very difficult to know how these independent voters, the largest voting bloc in the state, will vote. “They’re terribly unpredictable. They have no party loyalty. They move in and out of their independent status. They change their vote, so this key constituency in Arizona is hard to track,” he said. In Arizona, Trump won independents over former Vice President Kamala Harris by a 53-44 margin in 2024. In 2020, both Trump and former President Joe Biden won 48 percent of this constituency. Primaries tend to play to extremes, he said, so a central question facing Biggs is whether he will be able to chip away at Hobbs’ support among independent voters in November. “The real question is whether Biggs can pivot from his portrayal that he has as very MAGA and very conservative to attract some of the independents,” he said. Forecasters favor Hobbs. The Cook Political Report and Sabato’s Crystal Ball both classify the race as Lean Democratic. In March, Sabato’s Crystal Ball moved it away from a toss-up race, with analysts Kyle Kondik and J. Miles Coleman saying at the time that elsewhere, in New Jersey and Virginia, the 2025 governor races had seen Latino-heavy precincts that shifted rightward in 2024 vote more Democratic. “For Hobbs, a Latino reversion could lessen the generic GOP edge in Arizona, which reasserted itself in a major way in 2024 as Trump carried the state by 5.5 points after he narrowly lost it in 2020,” they wrote. They also noted that sitting governors not facing a major scandal are more difficult to defeat. Midterm elections are viewed as a referendum on the sitting president. Historically, the party in the White House loses seats in the midterms, so Trump's approval rating will loom over the race. Civiqs, which tracks Trump's approval rating in each state, found that 53 percent of Arizonans disapprove of Trump, compared to 42 percent who approve of him. What Prediction Markets Show Prediction markets favored Hobbs for reelection. Democrats had an 81 percent chance of victory on Kalshi and a 77 percent chance on Polymarket as Biggs won the primary on Tuesday. Prediction markets allow traders to buy and sell contracts tied to political outcomes and current events, aggregating real‑money wagers into probability estimates. Prices fluctuate as traders react to polling, fundraising, candidate developments and broader political trends. They measure trader sentiment at a given moment but do not always accurately predict the future. Arizona’s Purple State Status Arizona emerged as a key swing state over the past decade, with both parties scoring statewide victories. Trump won Arizona by about 5.5 points in the 2024 election, but on the same ballot, Democrat Ruben Gallego eked out a win in the Senate race, underscoring that Democrats are still competitive in the state. Republicans have continued to outpace Democrats in voter registration numbers in Arizona. As of July 2026, there were 1,523,886 registered Republicans and 1,210,510 registered Democrats in the state. An additional 1,572,158 Arizonans were registered with other parties or were not affiliated with one, according to the secretary of state’s office. Former President Joe Biden carried the state by under half a percentage point in 2020 against Trump. In 2016, Trump won Arizona by more than 3 points over former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton. Hobbs defeated Republican Kari Lake by less than a percentage point in the 2022 gubernatorial election. Democrats did well in other statewide offices that year—Senator Mark Kelly won reelection by nearly 5 points, and Democrats also won the attorney general and secretary of state races. Suburbs of Phoenix and Tucson will play a key role in the election. They shifted leftward for much of the 2010s as the state became more purple, but Trump did perform well in those areas in 2024. Margins among Hispanic and Latino voters will also be closely watched in November. Trump made inroads with these voter groups in 2024—one reason he managed to achieve such a relatively strong margin in Arizona. Former Vice President Kamala Harris won Latino voters in Arizona by 10 points (54 percent to 44 percent) in 2024, compared to Biden’s 24-point victory in 2020 (61 percent to 37 percent). But polls suggest the president’s approval rating has slipped among these groups.

center.title: Iran war has cost US $37.5bn so far, Hegseth says, as Pentagon seeks billions more

farLeftText: From 6h ago 18.50 EDT Houthi blockade 'likely to increase disruption' to Saudi oil exports To recap, the Houthis, who control northern and western Yemen including the coast at the mouth of the Red Sea, announced a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia on ⁠Monday. The announcement already appears to be having an impact. Two tankers which had just loaded Saudi crude bound for China and India at Saudi Arabia’s Red Sea port of Yanbu made U-turns on Tuesday, heading towards the Suez Canal rather ‌than out through the Bab el-Mandeb into the Indian Ocean. Assessing the situation, British maritime risk management group Vanguard said: double quotation mark The developments represent the first confirmed changes to commercial tanker routing following the (Houthi) embargo and are likely to increase disruption to Saudi crude exports and regional shipping patterns.” With the war shutting the strait of Hormuz, the Red Sea has served as the main alternate route out for millions of barrels of Saudi oil per day, diverted by pipeline through Yanbu. A vessel in the strait of Hormuz, as seen from Musandam, Oman. Photograph: Reuters Share Updated at 18.57 EDT 2h ago 23.09 EDT We’re closing this page now but you can read our latest full report here. A summary of the day’s biggest news is below. Thanks for following along. The US military said it completed the 11th night of strikes on Iran early on Wednesday, with targets including aircraft hangars and drone storage sites. In the Red Sea, Iran-backed Yemen’s Houthi rebels have opened a new front against Saudi Arabia by declaring a naval blockade of the kingdom. A news agency in Yemen that is run by the Houthis, SABA, said on Tuesday six ships in the Red Sea were forced to reroute after they received warnings from the rebel group. The claim could not be independently confirmed. Brent crude was trading up 1.2 percent at $92.09 a barrel at around 0130 GMT, its highest intraday price since 11 June. West Texas Intermediate (WTI), the main US oil benchmark, climbed 1.1 percent to $85.23 . Donald Trump said the US would soon target the area near Pickaxe Mountain – a fortified underground site near one of Iran’s primary nuclear enrichment facilities. The US war in Iran has cost $37.5bn so far, Pete Hegseth said , an increase of nearly $8bn since the last public estimate. The number of wounded US personnel in the Iran war has grown to well over 500 troops , a US official told the Associated Press. The US secretary of state Marco Rubio told Southeast Asian foreign ministers on Wednesday that Iranian control of the strait of Hormuz would set a dangerous precedent with repercussions beyond the Middle East . Trump said he would authorise the resumption of direct flights by US airlines to Lebanon, which have been suspended since the 1985 hijacking by Lebanese Islamist militants of a TWA flight with many Americans aboard. Share 2h ago 22.55 EDT Some more detail on the 11th night of US attacks on Iran: Iran’s state-run IRNA news agency reported that explosions rocked Bushehr, East Azerbaijan, Hamadan, Hormozgan, Khuzestan and Sistan and Baluchistan provinces. Share 3h ago 22.42 EDT The US secretary of state Marco Rubio told Southeast Asian foreign ministers on Wednesday that Iranian control of the strait of Hormuz would set a dangerous precedent with repercussions beyond the Middle East. double quotation mark If we create a precedent in the Middle East where a nation-state can decide to control an international waterway, charge a toll, and blow up your ships if you don’t pay, we have created a very dangerous precedent that will repeat itself in other parts of the world, including this region.” View image in fullscreen US secretary of state Marco Rubio speaks at the Asean post-ministerial conference. Photograph: Eloisa Lopez/EPA Share 3h ago 22.19 EDT Brent crude passes $92 a barrel, highest price since June Oil rose in Asian trade on Wednesday, with Brent crude hitting its highest price in more than a month. Brent crude was trading up 1.2% at $92.09 a barrel at around 0130 GMT, its highest intraday price since 11 June. West Texas Intermediate, the main US oil benchmark, climbed 1.1% to $85.23. Share Updated at 22.26 EDT 3h ago 22.01 EDT The US military has confirmed a third service member is believed to be dead in Friday’s attack on a base in Jordan and identified them as Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad, 28, of New York. The confirmation brings the death toll to 18 US service members killed since the war began. Share 4h ago 21.24 EDT Global trade could be disrupted by Houthi rebels at a second chokepoint Coming back now to the blockade of Saudi Arabia by Yemen’s Houthi rebels, and how it could affect oil and trade. The rebels say they have closed the Bab el-Mandeb strait to Saudi-linked shipping in retaliation for the kingdom’s blockade on Yemen and a recent attack on the international airport in Yemen’s rebel-held capital, Sanaa. The Houthis claim to have forced six ships to reroute on Tuesday, but there was no independent confirmation. “If Bab el-Mandeb comes under pressure at the same time as shipping in the Gulf, you have disruption around two of the world’s most important maritime routes at once,” says Clionadh Raleigh, the founder of ACLED, a conflict monitoring service. More than 7 million barrels of petroleum a day transited Bab el-Mandeb in June, compared to around 4 million before the Iran war, according to an analysis published on Monday by Allison Minor, the director of the Atlantic Council’s Project for Middle East Integration. View image in fullscreen Houthi military spokesman Yahya Sarea delivers a televised statement on 20 July. Photograph: Yahya Arhab/EPA Saudi Arabia has been recently exporting some 4 million barrels of oil a day through its Red Sea port of Yanbu, blunting the impact of the closure of the strait of Hormuz. Saudi could still get crude to market without using Bab el-Mandeb, by shipping up to 2.5 million barrels a day across the Red Sea to Egypt, whose SUMED pipeline can carry it to the Mediterranean. It could also ship 1 million barrels a day through the Suez Canal. International shippers could also reroute, as they did at the height of the war in Gaza. But that means going the long way around the Cape of Good Hope off South Africa, adding one or two weeks to voyages – and raising costs. Share 4h ago 20.59 EDT US Central Command (Centcom) has just provided some more information about vessels attacked by Iran in the strait of Hormuz and the flow of oil through the chokepoint. double quotation mark Over the past three months, Iran has attacked more than 30 commercial vessels transiting the international waterway vital for regional and global trade. The unwarranted attacks have endangered hundreds of innocent mariners and undermined freedom of navigation. Despite Iranian aggression, the Strait of Hormuz remains open for commercial vessel transit. Since early May, CENTCOM forces have helped facilitate the transit of approximately 900 commercial vessels and 450 million barrels of crude oil.” Share 5h ago 20.41 EDT US strikes completed for night US Central Command says its strikes for the night have been completed. The strikes included hitting aircraft hangars and drone storage sites, and lasted about an hour. We’ll have more details as we find out more. Share Updated at 20.52 EDT 5h ago 20.26 EDT Prior to today’s round of strikes, Iranian authorities say US attacks in just the past three weeks have killed at least 50 people and wounded more than 500 others. Both the US and Iran have targeted civilian infrastructure relied on by millions of people. Workers on ships, as well as foreign workers and others in Gulf nations, Israel and Lebanon have also been killed during the conflict. Share 5h ago 20.15 EDT Iran’s Fars news agency is reporting a US strike has hit a military site near Tabriz, the capital city of East Azerbaijan Province, in north-western Iran. Share 5h ago 20.09 EDT A little more detail on those US strikes: Iranian state media says air defence activity has been heard in western, eastern and north-eastern Tehran. The time in Tehran is 3.40am. Share 5h ago 20.02 EDT To recap some news from earlier today, Donald Trump said the US ⁠will ⁠be hitting ​the Pickaxe Mountain area, ⁠located near Iran’s heavily damaged ⁠Natanz uranium enrichment ​facility, “pretty ‌soon”. It is unclear if today’s US strikes are targeting Pickaxe Mountain. Pickaxe ‌Mountain is a ‌heavily fortified site that hosts two deeply buried tunnel complexes ‌that experts assess as beyond ​the reach of the most powerful bunker ⁠buster bombs in ​the US arsenal. ​The ​site is ​linked ‌to Iran’s ​nuclear ​program. View image in fullscreen A satellite image from 2 March, 2026 shows a closer view of the Natanz nuclear storage facility with new building damage Photograph: VANTOR/Reuters Iran’s military leaders said any attack on nuclear or “other sensitive facilities” would be considered a major escalation, according to a statement reported by Iranian state media. “We declare that if the invading and terrorist military of that country enters such a phase, we will regard it as an expansion of the war across the region, and all interests of the United States, its allies, and its supporters will become targets of powerful attacks by the Armed Forces of the Islamic Republic of Iran,” said the statement from the armed forces’ Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters. centerText:
The war with Iran has cost the US some $37.5bn (£28bn) so far - an increase of nearly $8bn since the last estimate in May, Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth has said.

Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee it was urgent that lawmakers approve another $87bn in congressional funding for the Pentagon, with $67bn of that destined for operations in the Middle East.

The hearing was disrupted several times by anti-war protesters and ended in a shouting match between the defence secretary and a senator.

It was Hegseth's first appearance on Capitol Hill since hostilities between the US and Iran restarted last week and comes after three American soldiers were confirmed killed in the region.

The White House asked Congress in April for $1.5tn for the Pentagon over the next fiscal year, which would raise American military spending to an all-time high in the modern era.

topic: House Shutdown Passes Spending Government Midterms

left.title: House passes spending bill to head off government shutdown before midterms

left-center.title: House Narrowly Passes Stopgap Spending Bill, but Shutdown Fight Looms

right-center.title: House GOP votes to punt government shutdown battle to after November midterms

farLeftText:
The US House of Representatives on Tuesday approved a stopgap measure to head off a government shutdown until early December, with its Republican leaders seeking to defuse a volatile issue in the run-up to the November midterm elections.

The continuing resolution authorizing government spending until 4 December passed by a vote of 220-205, roughly along party lines. It next heads to the Senate, where it will need to win bipartisan support to clear the chamber.

The government’s current spending authority expires on 30 September, but top Republicans had argued the measure’s passage was necessary to ensure that Democrats do not block government funding measures in the Senate.

They did so last year for a record 43 days in protest against the expiration of tax credits that lowered some healthcare costs, and twice again this year, to push for reforms to immigration enforcement operations after the killings of two US citizens in Minneapolis.

“Our conference refuses to let the obstruction and inaction of Senate Democrats trigger another manufactured shutdown at the end of September. That is why we are acting before a funding lapse crisis, not in response to it,” said Tom Cole, the House appropriations committee chair who spearheaded the resolution.

Earlier on Tuesday, lawmakers agreed to move forward on the stopgap measure and a budget framework paving the way for a $95bn party-line package, in a 214-211 vote roughly along party lines.

The constitution tasks Congress with authorizing government spending, and the Senate and House every year attempt to pass 12 appropriations measures granting those authorities. But lawmakers often miss those deadlines, forcing them to resort to passing continuing resolutions that extend governmental departments’ ability to operate.

While the House has passed three appropriations measures for the 2027 fiscal year beginning on 1 October, the Senate – where the top Democrat and Republican say they remain far apart in government spending talks – has approved none, nor have any advanced through committees, a key procedural step. Republican leaders ‌in the chamber said they were negotiating with Senate Democrats on their own short-term funding bill.

Rosa DeLauro, the Democratic ranking member on the House appropriations committee, denied that her party was seeking a shutdown, and called passage of a stopgap funding measure “necessary”.

However, she opposed the Republican-backed measure, calling it “hastily” conceived and noting it appropriated funds for the border patrol without “substantial reforms” to the agency that plays a major role in Donald Trump’s crackdown on undocumented immigrants.

“We are putting the cart before the horse by considering this legislation without understanding the full scope of what is needed to continue operations through December 4,” DeLauro said.

Should the Senate fail to pass the measure, funding negotiations will intensify from September, when Congress returns to Washington DC after spending most of August in recess with the end-of-the-month deadline to prevent a shutdown near.

Those talks could be complicated by the approaching midterm elections, in which Democrats hope they can retake control of the House and potentially the Senate, where they can only win a majority by picking up at least three seats in states that backed Trump two years ago.

NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles!

The House of Representatives voted on Tuesday night to punt the threat of another government shutdown until after the November midterm elections.

House lawmakers passed a bill to extend the current fiscal year's funding levels until Dec. 4 via a measure called a continuing resolution (CR). It would extend several weeks into fiscal year (FY) 2027, which begins on Oct. 1.

The legislation passed in a 220 to 205 vote. Just six House Democrats supported the measure compared to 204 who voted against. All but one House Republican supported the bill.

It's a victory for Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., and House Republicans, who are looking to avoid a messy federal spending fight right before what's expected to be an uphill battle to retain their razor-thin majority.

SENATE WEIGHS NEW, PAINFUL LEVERAGE TACTIC AS FEARS OF ANOTHER GOVERNMENT SHUTDOWN GROW

House Majority Whip Tom Emmer, R-Minn., the No. 3 House GOP leader, ripped the Democrats who opposed the measure.

"As the marxists and communists battle it out for control of the Democrat party, House Republicans are proving yet again that we are the party that governs and works for the American people. Under President Trump’s leadership, we are the party of commonsense. The other side — led by Commie Mamdani — is the party of chaos and destruction. A majority of Democrats just voted for another damning Schumer Shutdown," he said in reference to New York City's self-proclaimed Democratic Socialist Mayor Zohran Mamdani.

The current fiscal year saw the longest government shutdown in history, lasting from Oct. 1, 2025 until Nov. 12, 2025, as Senate Democrats blockaded GOP-led funding priorities for weeks in protest of spending cuts to federal healthcare programs.

And Republicans are warning that Democrats would almost certainly cause another government shutdown right before the election if the fight is not punted until after the early November midterms.

"Unfortunately, given that Democrats have already forced the two longest government shutdowns in American history in this Congress, one of them for 78 days, they've already demonstrated they don't care what kind of pain they inflict on the American people," Johnson told reporters on Tuesday. "They've shown there's nothing they'd love more than a government shutdown as tens of millions of Americans head to the polls in November."

House Democratic Caucus Chairman Pete Aguilar, D-Calif., told reporters Tuesday that he would vote against the bill along with the top Democrat on the House Appropriations Committee, the panel tasked with overseeing yearly federal spending.

"I think that there are some drafting errors within the CR that give Customs and Border Patrol flexibility to move money around. The administration said that all of that was funded within reconciliation 2.0. and I think they're creating other opportunities to transfer funds," Aguilar said.

But even with the House's victory, it's not clear that the fight will be over before September.

HOUSE GOP PUSHES BACK ON SENATE'S 'SKINNY' PLAN TO END RECORD-BREAKING DHS SHUTDOWN

The GOP-led Senate is working on its own course on funding extension, with sources familiar with the process telling Fox News Digital that appropriators were crafting their own CR. They believe the House’s product lacked key extensions to certain programs that are expected to run out of funding come the Sept. 30 deadline.

Those programs, known as anomalies, were shipped to the Senate from the White House on Monday.

Sen. John Hoeven, R-N.D., a member of the Senate Appropriations Committee, told Fox News Digital that Republicans and Democrats, for the time being, were working together to thwart a shutdown.

He hoped to get the CR onto the floor as soon as this week, given that the House is set to leave Washington, D.C., by the end of the week for a long August recess.

"Look, we appreciate the House moving forward with theirs. It's, you know, very clean," Hoeven said. "There are just a few issues that we think need to be the rest. Because, obviously, we need to get Democrats on board with us, right now, they're working with us."

CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP

The move has the full blessing of Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D.

"I think it's going to be really important that we figure out a path forward to get a bipartisan agreement to fund the government that will keep government open and ensure that government employees are getting paid," Thune said. "And that is something that I intend to bring to the floor for a vote before the August break.

farLeftText: Volodymyr Zelenskyy has fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparked nationwide protests. In a late-night TV address, Zelenskyy said he was appointing Mykhailo Drapatyi, a popular and innovative general, as the new head of Ukraine’s armed forces. Drapatyi would oversee a reorganisation of the army’s command structure, Ukraine’s president said. Zelenskyy said he was bringing Mykhailo Fedorov back into government after his removal as defence minister prompted thousands of people to demonstrate in Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities, in one of the biggest political crises since Russia’s 2022 invasion. View image in fullscreen Volodymyr Zelenskyy with Oleksandr Syrskyi at the handover of drone missiles to the Ukrainian army in Kyiv in December 2024. Photograph: Sergey Dolzhenko/EPA Ukraine’s president paid tribute to Syrskyi and credited him with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024, in which foreign tanks rolled on to Russian territory for the first time since the second world war. “We have come a long way. The defence of Ukraine continues. And every soldier deserves to be treated with respect,” Zelenskyy said, adding that he was grateful to Syrskyi and to “every one of our soldiers” fighting on the frontline. Zelenskyy will be hoping that his radical reshuffle of the army’s leadership will assuage protesters who have taken to the streets demanding Syrskyi’s removal. They had given a deadline of Friday for Fedorov to be reinstated and promised “indefinite protests” if their request was ignored. 1:32 Why Ukrainians are protesting against Zelenskyy’s dismissal of defence minister – video Fedorov welcomed Drapatyi’s appointment. “This brings a breath of fresh air and new hope to the struggle of free people for freedom and justice. It is a voice for change that could not be ignored,” he said. He added: “The high expectations of the Ukrainian people must be met with a clear vision for ending the war for independence, a strong team and decisive actions aimed at saving the lives of our soldiers, automating the frontline, delivering asymmetric strikes and crippling the Russian economy.” Drapatyi, the new commander-in-chief, is well-regarded within Ukraine’s army and is liked by the public. He is viewed as an ally of Fedorov, whose attempts to reform the military and force it to adopt modern technology and data led to a bitter clash with the old-school Syrskyi. A major general, Drapatyi is commander of Ukraine’s joint forces group. He has been fighting Russia since its covert attempt in 2014 to take over the eastern Donbas region. He became famous nationally when he drove an infantry fighting vehicle over a barricade set up by pro-Russian militants in the city of Mariupol. The Ukrainian media outlet Babel described Drapatyi as one of Ukraine’s most capable battlefield commanders. It said his new job would be fundamentally about reforming a large institution rather than commanding troops in combat. Zelenskyy said he and Drapatyi agreed that Ukraine’s long-range drone attacks on targets in Russia such as oil refineries would continue. “Our programme of precision strikes will be implemented with absolute precision,” he said, promising a smooth and coordinated handover following Syrskyi’s departure. He also said the new commander-in-chief would be tasked with updating the country’s defence strategy and reforming its structure. Another pressing issue is mobilisation, at a time when after nearly five years of full-scale war few Ukrainians are ready to volunteer. It is unclear what Fedorov’s new role will be, amid reports that he was insisting on being given his old job back as defence minister. This has now been filled by Yevhen Khmara, a former special operations commander. View image in fullscreen Yevhen Khmara replaced Mykhailo Fedeorov as Ukraine’s defence minister. Photograph: Ukrainian presidential press service/AFP/Getty Images Zelenskyy said he met Fedorov on Tuesday. “I offered Mykhailo a prominent position within the government that would enable him to unify the technological sector of our state and ensure its development,” he said, without giving further details. Zelenskyy added: “Our shared wish is one – victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace.” He said “a significant path has been travelled, the defence of Ukraine continues, and every warrior deserves dignified treatment”.
centerLeftText: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy fired his commander in chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi, on Tuesday, bowing to pressure after the removal of popular Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov sparked the fiercest leadership crisis of his presidency. Subscribe to read this story ad-free Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content. Zelenskyy announced on Telegram that Mykhailo Drapatyi will replace Syrskyi. Zelenskyy’s decision to fire Fedorov, one of his closest allies, led to rare wartime protests on the streets of Kyiv. Fedorov publicly aired his disagreements with Syrskyi, known as “The Butcher,” over military strategy. Syrskyi, 60, is a more traditional military commander focused on infantry and artillery, while Fedorov, 35, is seen as a modernizer, whose focus on technological advancements in drone warfare has led to Ukraine’s recent successes in hitting Russia. Despite the recent shake-up, Zelenskyy tried to present a unified front in his Telegram message Tuesday announcing the dismissal of Syrskyi. “The desire in all of us is one — victory over the enemy and achieving such conditions on the front and in pressure on Russia, which would allow forcing Russia to peace,” he wrote. Worried Ukrainians, fearing that Fedorov’s removal could wipe out recent gains, had taken to the streets calling for him to be reinstated and Syrskyi to be forced out, instead. A senior air force commander also quit in protest. For those in the streets, Syrskyi had become a symbol of the Soviet way of thinking rejected by Ukraine’s younger generations. Fedorov, meanwhile, represents technological innovation and a determination to outsmart the country’s more powerful invader. Syrskyi has been nicknamed “The Butcher” for his unsparing reputation, with critics pointing to lives lost in a late withdrawal from the fortress city of Bakhmut and the disastrous retreat from Russia’s Kursk region last year that left soldiers stranded and forced to walk back for days under enemy fire. Zelenskyy spent the weekend speaking to other top military commanders in an apparent interview process to potentially replace Syrskyi. It is unclear whether Fedorov will remain in the government and, if so, in what capacity. On Tuesday, Zelenskyy separately met with Fedorov and offered him what he called a respected position in government that would unify the country’s technology sector. But it was not clear whether Fedorov accepted the offer and whether the latest moves would satisfy protesters and bring peace to the streets. “Change is inevitable,” Fedorov said Saturday on social media. “The dialogue is happening. I believe we will succeed.” Former Ukrainian Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov holds a briefing in Kyiv on Thursday. Danylo Antoniuk / Future Publishing via Getty Images Zelenskyy did not give a reason for firing Fedorov last week. He said he was effectively left with a choice between the defense minister and Syrskyi, who was heading the armed forces. The two were no longer communicating with each other, he said. Fedorov confirmed his spat with Syrskyi, saying Syrskyi was not willing to discuss “problems” directly and resisted reform of Ukraine’s defense apparatus. 00:22 Protests erupt in Ukraine over defense minister's ouster 00:00 00:00 While he credited Syrskyi for Ukraine’s wins in the early days of the war, “the war has changed” because of drone advancements, Fedorov argued. “Why is this trajectory breaking now, and why are we veering off course? There is such a risk. And the Ukrainian people have taken to the streets for that,” he said at a news conference. Ukraine’s deployment of low-cost drones has upended the battlefield and the traditional model of warfare, with Kyiv demonstrating a growing ability to hit strategic sites deep inside Russia, in addition to critical Russian-held targets within Ukraine’s territory. At the same time, Russian troops continue to slowly advance in eastern Ukraine, while a dwindling supply of missile interceptors has left Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities vulnerable to intense bombardment.
centerText:
Ukraine's Zelenskyy fires his military chief after protests and names a new one

toggle caption Efrem Lukatsky/AP

KYIV, Ukraine (AP) — President Volodymyr Zelenskyy on Tuesday fired Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi as commander-in-chief of Ukraine's armed forces following days of mass protests in Kyiv and other cities nationwide demanding his removal.

Zelenskyy appointed Mykhailo Drapatyi as the new army chief, according to a statement on social media. "Our shared wish is one — victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace," Zelenskyy said.

The Ukrainian leader said he made the decision after a series of meetings with military commanders. They began shortly after protests broke out demanding the reinstatement of former Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov, seen by demonstrators as a driver of military modernization, and the removal of Syrskyi, who they said embodied the army's old, Soviet-style command culture.

Sponsor Message

Zelenskyy thanked Syrskyi for his achievements in Ukraine's fight against Russia and praised him for the defense of Kyiv and for operations in Kharkiv and Kursk. "I am grateful to Oleksandr Syrskyi and to every one of our warriors for Ukraine's strong front-line positions," he said. "I am grateful to Mykhailo Drapatyi for holding that same view."

On Tuesday, Zelenskyy separately met with Fedorov and offered him what he called a respected position in government that would unify the country's technology sector. But it wasn't clear if Fedorov accepted the offer and whether the latest moves would satisfy protesters and bring peace to the streets.

centerText:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has dismissed the country's army commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi.

It comes just days after the removal of popular Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov - a move that triggered street protests across the country.

Initial rumours that the end of Fedorov's tenure was related to a conflict between him and Syrskyi were soon confirmed.

Fedorov, 35, has been widely praised for energising the ministry and transforming the armed forces, as well as leading a drive against corruption. Syrskyi, 60, is seen by many in Ukraine as a Soviet-school commander reluctant to implement radical changes in the army.

Syrskyi defended his record in a farewell address, writing on Telegram: "An army that was fighting a difficult defensive campaign two years ago is now on the offensive."

"I am handing over to my successor an army that is not only holding the line but is advancing... I sincerely hope this offensive will continue."

Syrskyi was replaced by Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, who is a popular, battle-hardened general.

"I will work responsibly, with full focus, and with respect for the people who are defending our country today," Drapatyi said on Facebook.

He also thanked Syrskyi "for his consistent work to strengthen the Ukrainian army," adding: "I grew up in it."

In a video post on social media late on Tuesday, Zelensky thanked Syrskyi for his military campaigns, including his role in successfully defending the capital Kyiv during the first days of Russia's full-scale invasion in 2022.

Zelensky also revealed that earlier in the day, he met Fedorov and offered him a "worthy position in power", but did not say whether the offer had been accepted.

Fedorov was last week replaced by Yevhenii Khmara, a former top official at Ukraine's Security Service (SBU).

"All decisions will be formalised tomorrow," Zelensky added in his post.

centerRightText:
See more of our coverage in your search results.

A 34-year-old Australian mom has given birth to a rare set of identical quadruplets after a naturally conceived pregnancy that doctors say occurs in about 1 in 15 million cases.

Jenitar Na’amoana welcomed her “precious miracles” Emily, Harriet, Catherine and Alexa by cesarean section at 28 weeks and four days on July 14 at Royal Brisbane and Women’s Hospital in Herston, Queensland, the BBC reported.

Na’amoana and her husband Jortham were taken by “immense surprise” when they learned they were expecting identical quadruplets despite not using fertility treatments.

4 A 34-year-old Australian mom welcomed rare identical quadruplets conceived naturally — a birth estimated at 1 in 15 million. 9 News Australia

The babies are monozygotic quadruplets, meaning they developed after a single fertilized egg split into four embryos.

The Brisbane couple are now parents of eight children after the birth, with their older kids ranging from 1 to 10 years old.

Dr. Alexa Bendall, who oversaw Na’amoana’s care, said the case was even more unusual because all four babies shared a single placenta.

“Identical quadruplets occur at one in every 15 million pregnancies, but to share the same placenta — this is unheard of,” Bendall told the BBC.

Na’amoana was admitted to the hospital at 25 weeks so doctors could closely monitor both her and the babies because of the risks associated with the pregnancy.

4 Jenitar Na’amoana and her husband Jortham grew their family from four children to eight with the quadruplets. 9 News Australia

Despite those risks, Na’amoana reached 28 weeks and four days without experiencing the major complications doctors had anticipated.

“We always said that if we could get her to 28 weeks, we would be doing well,” Bendall said.

“So the fact that we’ve got four beautiful, healthy babies born at 28 weeks and four days is incredible and we’re very happy to be a part of it.”

4 The babies developed after one fertilized egg split into four embryos — a rare occurrence. 9 News Australia

Na’amoana “has somehow dodged every complication and risk for both herself and her babies,” Bendall said.

Na’amoana and her husband named one of the newborns Alexa in honor of Bendall. The doctor called the gesture “a beautiful tribute,” the ABC reported.

The newborns are expected to remain in the neonatal intensive care unit for several weeks while they continue to grow stronger.

4 The four girls remain in the neonatal intensive care unit as they continue to grow stronger after being born prematurely. 9 News Australia

Na’amoana later launched a GoFundMe campaign to help cover medical expenses and the cost of a larger van for the family’s new life as a household of 10.

“Our four precious miracles have arrived safely,” she wrote. “And while our hearts are overflowing with gratitude, the reality of caring for four newborns at once has brought challenges we never could have imagined.”

As of July 21, the fundraiser had raised more than 82,000 Australian dollars (about $57,500).

farRightText: Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announced Tuesday that the Trump administration is deferring more than $1 billion in Medicaid payments to Democrat-led California and Minnesota over high-risk claims flagged in a crackdown on fraud. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) is holding back roughly $867.5 million from California and $199 million from Minnesota, HHS said in a news release. The department stressed the money is being deferred, not cut, and that both states can recover it by documenting that the claims meet federal Medicaid requirements. “States that receive federal Medicaid funding must demonstrate that every dollar meets federal requirements,” Kennedy said. “When they cannot, we will not release federal funds until they do.” At a news conference, the health secretary named Govs. Gavin Newsom and Tim Walz directly, saying all they had to do was provide basic documentation showing the services were legitimate, which he called “common sense.” He added that the administration used artificial intelligence and advanced analytics to identify the suspicious spending. CMS Administrator Dr. Mehmet Oz was blunter. “CMS is done trying to chase down stolen and misused funds after they’ve already left the building,” Oz said, adding that stopping fraud “before the check clears” was delivering record-high savings for taxpayers. The department said its review flagged in-home care programs in California where spending growth far exceeded national trends, along with claims in 14 high-risk service areas in Minnesota tied to providers previously flagged through program integrity reviews. Under Kennedy’s direction, HHS and its Office of Inspector General are also using exclusion authorities to remove bad actors from Medicare and Medicaid and, in many cases, permanently bar them from returning. The deferrals follow an April takedown in which the Justice Department charged eight defendants in a $50 million health care fraud scheme dubbed “Operation Never Say Die,” including operators of sham hospices that billed Medicare for beneficiaries who were not terminally ill. According to court documents, one Anaheim operator recorded a non-death discharge rate of roughly 85 percent, nearly five times the national average, while paying beneficiaries cash kickbacks delivered in envelopes. The action also marks the second time the administration has moved against the two states, after Vice President JD Vance announced a $259 million Minnesota pause in February as part of a national “war on fraud.” Walz claimed the move amounted to political retribution, arguing the administration was punishing children, seniors, and people with disabilities rather than fraudsters. The U.S. attorney in Minnesota has estimated total fraud in the state could exceed $1 billion.

left.title: US military identifies third service member killed in Iranian attack on Jordan base

left-center.title: U.S. Military Identifies 2 Soldiers Killed In Iranian Attack On Jordan

farLeftText:
The US military has confirmed that a third service member is believed to be dead in Friday’s attack on a base in Jordan and identified them as Sgt Angel S Rampersad, 28, of New York.

The confirmation brings the death toll to 18 US service members killed since the war began.

On Monday, the Pentagon identified 1stLt Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Pvt Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas, as the two other soldiers who were killed in Jordan in Friday’s attacks. The soldiers were killed by Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks in the first troop deaths from direct Iranian fire since the opening days of the war. They were deployed to support a US mission against the Islamic State group, a US central command statement said.

After the attack, US central command announced that two service members were killed and another was missing. The following day, the military command said that troops found unidentified remains and that an examination process to verify the remains was under way.

Now, the Pentagon says that Rampersad is believed to be that missing soldier and her official status is now “Duty Status-Whereabouts Unknown”, and she is “believed to be deceased”.

Rampersad belonged to the same unit as Gonzales – the 10th Army Air + Missile Defense Command based in Ansbach, Germany.

Another US soldier killed in Iraq during a controlled detonation of an Iranian drone was identified as Sgt Michael Emmanuel Swinton, a 30-year-old father from North Carolina who had served for nearly a decade, according to a military statement and his family.

Renewed fighting between the US and Iran is now in its 10th day, with both sides launching barrages of strikes. Iran has attacked US allies in the region, and American soldiers are stationed across the Middle East.

Iranian authorities say US strikes in just the past three weeks have killed at least 50 people and wounded more than 500 others.

Both sides have targeted civilian infrastructure relied on by millions of people. Workers on ships, as well as foreign workers and others in Gulf nations, Israel and Lebanon, have also died in the conflict.
centerLeftText:
LOADING ERROR LOADING

WASHINGTON (AP) — The Trump administration on Monday identified two soldiers killed in action last week as part of the U.S. war with Iran.

The Defense Department said in a statement that 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas, were killed in Jordan, where they were supporting a U.S. mission against the Islamic State. It’s unclear how they were killed.

Advertisement

On Saturday, the U.S. Central Command had two service members were killed in Jordan while defending against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks.

The U.S. military has said the total number of deaths in the war with Iran is 17.

The deaths reflect the complicated reality that American boots don’t need to be on the ground for there to be lethal risks in a conflict that involves drones, missiles and airplanes. American forces are arrayed across the Middle East, making other nations targets of Iran as the fighting has escalated after a breakdown in peace talks.

President Donald Trump has said the war is necessary to stop Iran from developing nuclear weapons.

First Fatalities Came Soon After The War Started

Shortly after the war began on Feb. 28, an Iranian drone strike at a civilian port in Kuwait killed six American soldiers. The soldiers were part of a supply and logistics unit based in Iowa who were working at a shipping container-style building that had no defenses.

Advertisement

A seventh soldier died more than a week after being wounded during a March 1 attack by Iran on the Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia.

Later in March, six service members were killed when a KC-135 refueling aircraft supporting U.S. military operations against Iran crashed in Iraq. The aircraft was in “friendly” airspace when an unspecified incident involving another aircraft took place, according to U.S. Central Command.

On Monday, the U.S. military said a Navy pilot died in a helicopter crash in the Arabian Sea. The Navy initially described the July 1 crash as an emergency landing and said there was “no indication the emergency was caused by hostile action.” The remaining three sailors aboard the helicopter were rescued.

Advertisement

Drones And Missiles Still Claiming Lives

The latest U.S. military death from the conflict was on Saturday in northern Iraq when an American service member in northern Iraq was killed during a “controlled detonation” of unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian drone, U.S. Central Command said Sunday. A second service member was wounded and was receiving medical treatment for a minor injury.

The military said it was withholding additional information until 24 hours after families had been notified.

The deaths in the war are not limited to Americans.

Iranian authorities said at least 50 people have been killed and more than 500 wounded in U.S. strikes in the past three weeks, including eight killed in a strike on a bridge Friday.

Unlike 125 other countries, the United States is not a state party to the Rome Statute, which established the ICC, and so is not obligated to arrest indicted war crimes suspects. Last week, the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, announced “a sweeping campaign to dismantle the threat posed by the International Criminal Court to U.S. sovereignty” and promised to “systematically disable the ICC’s ability to operate”. The arrest of accused war crimes suspects has tested notions of sovereignty in international law in the past. In 1998, the former Chilean dictator Augusto Pinochet was arrested while visiting London on the orders of the Spanish judge Baltasar Garzón who sought Pinochet’s extradi­tion to Spain to face charges of genocide, terrorism and torture. Pinochet was was detained for 16 months before the UK’s home secretary decide that he was too ill to stand trial and allowed him to return home on compassionate grounds. In 1960, Adolf Eichmann, the Nazi official who organized the deportation of millions of European Jews to death camps, was secretly arrested by Israeli commandos in Argentina, where he had been living under an assumed name. At the Senate hearing, which has now concluded, senator Dick Durbin, an Illinois Democrat, asked Hegseth to explain why the Pentagon was asking Congress for an additional $67bn to pay for the war Trump started on 28 February when “the Department of Defense still has $75bn in unobligated funding from last year’s reconciliation package”. US defense secretary Pete Hegseth said that the US war on Iran had cost the Pentagon $37.5 “That 75,” Hegseth replied, “there’s plans and designs on that part of the rebuild of our military which had to be undertaken.” Hegseth then launched into a deeply partisan attack on his predecessor, Lloyd Austin, the retired army general, for supposedly pursuing “woke priorities” instead of military readiness. “I don’t know what Lloyd Austin did for four years, I really don’t,” he went on. “The amount of neglect, the amount of stale thinking and bureaucracy proceed staggering when we took over.” “Let me interrupt your speech for a minute, Mr Secretary,” Durbin cut in. “You estimated at one point that the cost of the war in Iran was $29bn, but then added a caveat that there was a replacement of munitions, for example. Do you have a new estimate about what the war has cost us so far in this the fifth month of the first year of this war in Iran?” the senator asked. “Senator, the estimate we have, as of today, is $37.5bn,” Hegseth answered. Pressed further by Durbin, Hegseth said that was the estimated cost of the war on Iran through the end of the US government’s financial year on 30 September. The states entrusted to run our elections are best positioned to detect and correct these errors, as they have access to substantially better data than the federal government,” Becker said. “As CEIR’s own research affirms, noncitizen registration remains extremely rare, and noncitizen voting is infinitesimally rare. In this case, the number of wrongful registrations represents a miniscule fraction, roughly 0.1%, of New Jersey’s total active voter registration records in 2024,” he continued. “The number of potential ballots cast from those incorrect registrations would represent roughly 0.009% of all ballots cast in New Jersey’s 2024 general election, when Kamala Harris defeated President Trump by more than 250,000 votes,” Becker added. “Joe Biden actually defeated Trump by a margin three times as large before this error took place.” Share Updated at 18.23 EDT 8h ago 17.09 EDT Hegseth refuses to say that US military has ability to destroy Iranian facility buried beneath Pickaxe mountain At the ongoing Senate Appropriations Committee hearing, senator John Kennedy, a Louisiana Republican, pressed US defense secretary Pete Hegseth to say whether the US military could bomb a fortified underground site linked to Iran’s nuclear program known as Pickaxe mountain, which the US has not yet targeted. “We need straight answers,” Kennedy said with some exasperation. “Do we have the ability to destroy whatever is underneath Pickaxe mountain?” “Senator, a lot of our capabilities are classified, but if anybody in the world can reach anything on God’s given, God’s green Earth, it’s the United States military,” Hegseth replied. centerText: Trump hits Canada with slew of new 50% tariffs set to take effect in 30 days Transcript JUANA SUMMERS, HOST: The White House announced new 50% tariffs on a wide array of Canadian goods this week. It ratchets up the tension between the two countries, all while they and Mexico are set to renegotiate the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement - or USMCA trade pact. NPR White House correspondent Danielle Kurtzleben is here to explain it all. Hi there. DANIELLE KURTZLEBEN, BYLINE: Hey there. SUMMERS: Danielle, start by telling us about the tariffs. What is being tariffed and why? KURTZLEBEN: Well, the White House has been straightforward in saying these new tariffs are a way of getting back at Canada. Here was U.S. trade representative Jamieson Greer on CNBC today. (SOUNDBITE OF ARCHIVED RECORDING) JAMIESON GREER: So the types of things we're doing are overdue. No one has retaliated against us, except China and Canada. So we're taking a targeted measure to counter that retaliation. KURTZLEBEN: So in other words, the U.S. tariffed Canadian goods. Canada retaliated, and now the U.S. is itself retaliating, using a section of a 1930 law that's never been used for tariffs before. And in that spirit of retaliation, some of these tariffs are at least somewhat tit for tat. Some Canadian provinces have banned the sale of U.S. alcoholic beverages, for example. So now the U.S. wants to tariff Canadian alcohol. Canada has also long had these restrictive quotas on dairy imports, and that was even before Trump and his tariffs. So here, the U.S. is tariffing some Canadian dairy goods. But importantly, these new tariffs aren't in effect yet. That happens a month from now. SUMMERS: Now, Danielle, you said that some tariffs are tit for tat. What other goods are targeted? KURTZLEBEN: Well, in response to Canadian tariffs on U.S. cars, the administration has a wide range of products they're targeting. There are some types of plywood, essential oils, even tulip bulbs. I mean, really, across these different tariff proclamations, there are long, very specific lists of goods. For example, digging through, I noticed a few very Canadian pieces of sports equipment being tariffed - hockey sticks, ice skates and fishing rods. But that brings me to something important. There's a wide range of goods being tariffed and at 50%, which is high, but it's still not a huge amount of goods. SUMMERS: OK. So how much is being tariffed? KURTZLEBEN: Well, Greer's office said that this covers $20 billion worth of goods, which is around 5% of Canadian exports to the U.S. And really, if you look at the biggest exports to the U.S., many of those were already being tariffed or they're specifically carved out here, like energy and potash - that's a type of fertilizer. And the administration has already tariffed aluminum and some lumber and furniture from all over the world. Now, that doesn't mean these tariffs aren't meaningful. Specific industries and businesses could be very much hurt by a 50% tariff. SUMMERS: So why is the Trump administration doing this? KURTZLEBEN: You know, I asked Kathleen Claussen this. She's a professor at Georgetown University law school, and her answer was simple. KATHLEEN CLAUSSEN: I think about them as leverage in the USMCA negotiations because these are areas that have been irritants for the bilateral relationship. KURTZLEBEN: Now, the first Trump administration, you may remember, negotiated the USMCA as a replacement for NAFTA, and now they're likely hoping these new tariffs will help them get concessions. And again, there's a month until these go into effect. SUMMERS: I do have to ask you, though, about the politics here. I mean, this is all happening while Americans are worried about the economy, as the Iran war keeps gas prices high, and, of course, tariffs raise prices too. So politically speaking, why would the administration do this? KURTZLEBEN: You know, it's hard to say because they haven't explicitly talked about the politics. Now, again, they have said this is to get back at Canada. And also, if these tariffs work as leverage, Trump may be able to claim some sort of a win in USMCA negotiations. But this is not the end. Earlier this year, the Supreme Court, of course, struck down a bunch of Trump's tariffs, and he's continuing to try to replace them using multiple other laws. And some of those new tariffs may be coming very, very soon. SUMMERS: NPR White House correspondent Danielle Kurtzleben. Thank you. KURTZLEBEN: Yeah. Thank you. Copyright © 2026 NPR. topic: Mamdani Arrest Netanyahu Authority Lacks Urges

left.title: Mamdani says New York City does not have legal authority to arrest Netanyahu

left-center.title: Mamdani says he won't try to arrest Israel's Netanyahu

right-center.title: Mamdani says he lacks authority to arrest Netanyahu but urges Trump to do so

farLeftText: Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, acknowledged on Tuesday he does not have the authority to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu, but urged US federal authorities to do so if the Israeli prime minister visits the US later this year. “Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” Mamdani said in a video posted to social media. Mamdani said he believes the international criminal court warrant for Netanyahu’s arrest over alleged war crimes is justified, but admitted that he lacks the legal authority to enforce it. “My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City could execute the international criminal court’s arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here,” Mamdani said. “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant. “The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant,” the mayor added. “And I want to be equally clear: Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome in New York City, nor is any other war criminal at large.” Netanyahu is expected to travel to New York in September for the United Nations general assembly. Mamdani said last week he was reviewing whether he would be able to arrest Netanyahu during that visit, after pledging to do so during his mayoral campaign last year. “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani said in reference to the Dutch city where the international criminal court, which issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in 2024, is located. The US does not recognize the ICC’s authority and is not a party to it. His remarks drew rebuke from Donald Trump, who insisted Netanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil. Trump said in a social media post Netanyahu is “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”. Netanyahu, meanwhile, said he’s “not concerned” about Mamdani’s comments.
centerLeftText: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani conceded late Tuesday that the city does not have the authority to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. Mamdani had vowed during his mayoral campaign last year that if Netanyahu visited New York City, his administration would enforce an International Criminal Court arrest warrant accusing the Israeli leader of committing war crimes during Israel's war against Hamas in the Gaza Strip. Mamdani did not back down in recent days, saying the city would "follow all applicable local laws" if Netanyahu visited for the United Nations General Assembly this fall. But in a Tuesday evening social media video, Mamdani said the city had concluded it could not seek the Israeli prime minister's arrest. "My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City could execute the International Criminal Court's arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here," Mamdani said. "It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant." Mamdani called on the federal government to arrest Netanyahu instead, calling him a "war criminal" and the "architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people." Israeli Ambassador to the United Nations Danny Danon condemned the video, writing on social media: "Zohran Mamdani, ENOUGH. Enough with the blood libels." "You were elected to serve New Yorkers, not Hamas' propaganda," he wrote. President Trump wrote Monday on Truth Social that Netanyahu "will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form, while in the United States of America." CBS News has reached out to Netanyahu's office and the White House for comment. The United States is not a party to the global treaty that created the International Criminal Court, and generally does not recognize its authority. A prosecutor for the ICC applied for arrest warrants against Netanyahu, former Israeli Defense Minister Yoav Gallant and several Hamas officials in May 2024, following an investigation into both Hamas' Oct. 7, 2023, terror attack on Israel and the Israeli military's response in Gaza. About 1,200 people in Israel were killed in the Oct. 7 attack and 250 others were taken hostage, while over 73,000 Palestinians have been killed in Gaza, the Hamas-run Gaza Health Ministry says. Judges for the court issued arrest warrants for Netanyahu and Gallant later in 2024, finding "reasonable grounds" to believe that the two Israeli officials deprived the civilian population of food, medicine and other humanitarian aid. Former Hamas commander Mohammed Deif, who was killed in an Israeli airstrike, was also accused of crimes against humanity. Israel has strongly condemned the warrants, with Netanyahu's office saying in 2024 it "rejects with disgust the absurd and false actions and charges against it by the International Criminal Court, which is a biased and discriminatory political body." The Israeli government has long defended its conduct in the Gaza Strip, arguing it has tried to protect civilians during its intense aerial and ground operations and blaming Hamas for operating in densely populated areas. Hamas and Israel agreed to a ceasefire last fall, though some operations have continued, and the Gaza Health Ministry has said more than 1,000 people have died during the ceasefire.

centerRightText: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani said on Tuesday that his office lacks the power to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu when he visits the Big Apple later this year but urged President Donald Trump to carry out the International Criminal Court‘s warrant. In a two-minute video posted to social media, Mamdani backed down from his plans to have Netanyahu arrested during a trip to New York City for the U.N.’s annual meeting in September. “My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City could execute the International Criminal Court’s arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here,” Mamdani said. “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant. The federal government, however, does, and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this one. And I want to be equally clear: Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome in New York City, nor is any other war criminal at large.” Advertisement Mamdani, a socialist and critic of Israel, called Netanyahu an “architect of genocide” and cited what he alleged were crimes committed by the Israeli prime minister in Gaza, including the killing of thousands of Palestinians, withholding humanitarian aid, and targeting hospitals. “Anyone, with their eyes, with their heart, with their conscience, should recognize the devastation he has wrought and understand that he belongs before a court of law,” he said. The ICC’s arrest warrant for Netanyahu charges him with allegations of war crimes and crimes against humanity, which Netanyahu strongly rejects. Advertisement While it is unlikely Trump would grant Mamdani’s request, it is even more doubtful the president would carry out a warrant from the ICC, as it is not held in high regard by the United States. Following Mamdani’s pledge to arrest Netanyahu, Trump jumped to the prime minister’s defense on social media. “He is fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran, which recently killed 52,000 innocent protestors, and has spent the last 47 years killing American Soldiers, and others,” Trump said. “The only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented SPIRAL OF DEATH AND DESTRUCTION, something that should have been dealt with years ago, by previous Presidents!” HERE’S WHY MAMDANI CAN’T ARREST NETANYAHU DESPITE SUGGESTING OTHERWISE Advertisement Although Mamdani backed down, anti-Israel sentiment is spreading in Congress. Last week, over half of House Democrats voted to block long-standing U.S. aid to Israel, favoring an amendment sponsored by Rep. Thomas Massie (R-KY) to cut off all military assistance to the country. Mamdani-minded socialists are also on the rise in the 2026 elections, with a handful of candidates having won in Democratic primaries across the country with platforms staunchly against Israel.
